text: When designing a REST API, following best practices ensures it is easy to use and maintain. First, use clear and logical endpoints. For example, `/users` is better than `/getUsers`. Second, use standard HTTP status codes to indicate success or errors, such as 200 for success, 404 for not found, and 500 for server errors. This helps developers quickly understand responses. Documentation is also crucial; tools like Swagger can make your API easy to test and understand. Finally, include versioning in your endpoints, like `/v1/users`, to avoid breaking changes when you update the API. These practices lead to a more reliable and developer-friendly API.
